coredata.dc:title: Dual targeting of Mg/N doped-carbon quantum dots with folic and hyaluronic acid for targeted drug delivery and cell imaging

Yaghoobi, Elnaz
coredata.dc:description: Mg/N doped-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with dual drug targeting and cell imaging properties was synthesized. Mg/N doped-CQDs synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Operating pyrolysis parameters such as temperature, time, and pH were optimized to achieve CQDs with high quantum yield (QY). This CQD applied in cellular imaging. For the first time, dual active targeting of Mg/N doped CQDs performed using folic acid and hyaluronic acid (CQD-FA-HA). Then, epirubicin (EPI) loaded on this nanocarrier as the final complex (CQD-FA-HA-EPI). Cytotoxicity analysis, cellular uptake, and cell photography performed for the complex on three cell lines, including 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO. In vivo studies were performed in BALB/c inbred female mice models bearing breast cancer. Characterization results showed the successful formation of Mg/N doped-CQDs with a high QY of 89.44%. In vitro drug release approved pH dependency of synthesized nanocarrier with a controlled release behavior. Cytotoxicity tests and cellular uptake results demonstrated increased toxicity and absorption into 4T1 and MCF-7 cell lines for targeted nanoparticles compared to free drug. In cell imaging, an increase in the entry of the complex into 4T1 and MCF-7 cells compared to free drug, confirmed the proper function of the synthesized complex. In vivo results indicated that the tumor volume of mice receiving CQD-FA-HA-EPI was the lowest among other studied groups, along with the lowest damage to the liver, spleen, and heart according to the histopathological analysis. Finally, CQD-FA-HA proposed as a novel platform with tumor targeting, drug carrier, and photoluminescence properties.

head.ce:title: Dual targeting of Mg/N doped-carbon quantum dots with folic and hyaluronic acid for targeted drug delivery and cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mg/N doped-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with dual drug targeting and cell imaging properties was synthesized. Mg/N doped-CQDs synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Operating pyrolysis parameters such as temperature, time, and pH were optimized to achieve CQDs with high quantum yield (QY). This CQD applied in cellular imaging. For the first time, dual active targeting of Mg/N doped CQDs performed using folic acid and hyaluronic acid (CQD-FA-HA). Then, epirubicin (EPI) loaded on this nanocarrier as the final complex (CQD-FA-HA-EPI). Cytotoxicity analysis, cellular uptake, and cell photography performed for the complex on three cell lines, including 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO. In vivo studies were performed in BALB/c inbred female mice models bearing breast cancer. Characterization results showed the successful formation of Mg/N doped-CQDs with a high QY of 89.44%. In vitro drug release approved pH dependency of synthesized nanocarrier with a controlled release behavior. Cytotoxicity tests and cellular uptake results demonstrated increased toxicity and absorption into 4T1 and MCF-7 cell lines for targeted nanoparticles compared to free drug. In cell imaging, an increase in the entry of the complex into 4T1 and MCF-7 cells compared to free drug, confirmed the proper function of the synthesized complex. In vivo results indicated that the tumor volume of mice receiving CQD-FA-HA-EPI was the lowest among other studied groups, along with the lowest damage to the liver, spleen, and heart according to the histopathological analysis. Finally, CQD-FA-HA proposed as a novel platform with tumor targeting, drug carrier, and photoluminescence properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request. The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is the most common cancer among women worldwide and the second most common cancer overall [1]. Treatment options for breast cancer include surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, hormone therapy, and targeted therapy [2]. There are various chemotherapy drugs. Anthracyclines are a group of drugs used in chemotherapy for various types of cancers, such as breast cancer. They are extracted from the bacterium Streptomyces peucetius [3]. Two essential and commonly used anthracyclines, doxorubicin (DOX) and epirubicin (EPI), are the basis of many chemotherapies in early clinical trials of breast cancer [4].

EPI with the chemical formula C27H29NO11 and molecular mass of 543.525 g·mol−1 is a 4′-epimer of doxorubicin and is a derivative of daunorubicin. It differs from doxorubicin in the reorientation (epimerization) of the hydroxyl group at position 4′ of the daunosamine ring [4]. The mechanism of action of EPI is similar to that of doxorubicin and other anthracycline drugs. EPI first forms a complex with DNA by placing its planar rings between nucleotide base pairs and stops DNA replication through DNA cleavage by topoisomerase II. Then, EPI stabilizes the topoisomerase II-DNA complex, followed by irreversible DNA strand breaks and cell death. EPI is also capable of generating toxic free radicals that are highly reactive against DNA, cell membranes, and mitochondria [3]. In some ongoing studies, EPI is preferred over DOX because it produces fewer side effects in addition to lower cardiotoxicity. EPI has a different spatial orientation of the hydroxyl group at carbon 4, which causes the opposite chirality. This causes faster elimination and reduces toxicity of the drug in the body [4].

The most advanced approach in drug delivery is the active targeting mechanism. Active targeting depends on specific ligands or molecules, such as transferrin and folate, that bind to molecules or receptors in tumor cells. This type of targeting is called ligand-mediated targeting [5]. Nanoparticles (NPs) containing ligands with specific functions such as retention and absorption should be placed near the target to have a higher affinity with cancer cells. This strategy increases the modification of nanoparticles attached to the cancer cells and drug penetration [6]. A critical mechanism of ligand-mediated targeting is ligand recognition by cancer cell receptors [7]. Ligands include proteins, peptides, antibodies, nucleic acids, sugars, hyaluronic acid, and small molecules such as vitamins and folic acid [8].

Folic acid (FA) or vitamin B9 is vital in nucleotide synthesis. It has a non-peptide part with a low molecular weight (441 Da) [9]. The affinity of FA to folate receptors is approximately 1 nM, which is very high compared to other receptors [10].
Hyaluronic acid is a hydrophilic polymer mainly found in epithelial, neural, and connective tissue. It comprises non-sulfated glycosaminoglycan, the most common or primary substrate of CD44 [11].

The absorption of FA and HA happens via folate and CD44 receptors, respectively, through endocytosis mediated by clathrin and CD44. Drug nanocarriers containing FA and HA are covalently bound to the surface of the carcinoma cells by the receptors. By decreasing the pH inside the endocytic vesicles, the drug bound to NP. Therefore, FA and HA can directly deliver the therapeutic agent to cancerous cells in a controlled manner and reduce the harmful effects of chemotherapeutic drugs on normal cells [12].

Carbon quantum dots)CQDs) are a new generation of carbon nanostructures of fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphenes. These CQDs have passivation surfaces with much attention due to their high stability to light radiation, biocompatibility, low toxicity, economic efficiency, and abundant raw materials in nature. Generally, CQDs contain a large number of carboxylic acid groups on their surface. For this reason, they have excellent solubility in water and are suitable for functionalization by various organic, polymeric, and biological species [13].

In this study, CQDs applied to design a targeted drug delivery system for EPI drug. At first, CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method. In order to achieve CQDs with high QY, the operating conditions optimized. Then, modification of the surface performed using FA and HA. Finally, the EPI drug loaded on the NP. After approving the cytotoxicity of the synthesized nanocarrier on cancerous cells, its efficacy in vivo assessed in BALB/c inbred female mice bearing breast cancer cells. However, the main objectives of this study can be summarized as follows:
✓
CQDs synthesis with high QY and surface modification with Mg and N atoms.
✓
Purification of CQDs with a novel and simple method using a centrifuge, passing through Al2O3 fixed bed and dialysis process.
✓
Surface modification of CQDs with FA and HA.
✓
Examining the cell imaging ability of the nanocarrier.
✓
Investigating the performance of this novel nanocarrier in vitro, ex vivo, and in vivo in BALB/c inbred female mice.

CQD synthesis: Citric acid (CA), magnesium hydroxide (MgOH), ethylenediamine (EDA), folic acid (FA), and hyaluronic acid (HA) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). EPI hydrochloride obtained from Euroasia Co., Ltd. (India). N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), Neutral red (NR), and aluminum oxide (Al2O3) were purchased from Merck (Germany).

Cell culture: Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7), mouse breast cancer cells (4T1), and Epithelial cells derived from the Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) obtained from the Pasteur Institute of Iran. Cells cultured in RPMI 1640 (Gibco) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (Sigma-Aldrich).

Animals: BALB/c inbred female mice (6–8 weeks of age, 18–20 g) purchased from the Razi Vaccine and Serum Research Institute, North East Branch, Mashhad, Iran. Animals kept in average humidity of 60 ± 5% and a controlled temperature of 25 ± 2°C at specified-pathogen-free conditions. To protect the animals from pain and discomfort, experiments carried out according to the ethical protocols approved by the Ferdowsi University of Mashhad.

UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence were obtained by UV-1800 (Shimadzu, Japan) and RF-540 (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Dried samples dispersed in deionized water in the specific concentration. Then, UV vis and fluorescence were performed.

Surface charge was investigated by zeta potential analysis using a Zeta Compact device (CAD, France). In this analysis, after synthesis and purification of the sample, it was diluted with a ratio of 1–50, and the zeta analysis was performed at pH= 7.4 and 25 °C.

To determine the morphology and structure of CQDs, Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were taken by MIRA3 (TESCAN) and CM120 (Philips), respectively. HR-TEM (TEC9G20) taken to examine the morphology on a finer scale and obtain the distance between graphene sheets of CQDs. In FESEM imaging, sample put on the grid to dry. Then, it was coated with a thin layer of gold and the image was taken. For TEM and HRTEM imaging, first, samples were homogenized using an ultrasonic bath, placed on a copper grid and imaging was done.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis used to obtain the crystallographic structure of CQDs. Dried samples were applied and X-ray diffractometer was performed at 40 kV and 15° < 2Ө < 60°.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis performed to obtain functional groups on the CQDs surface using AVATAR 370 FTIR device. In this experiment, a certain amount of dried sample was mixed with KBr powder and turned into tablets. The tablet placed in the device compartment, and FTIR curve plotted in the 4000–500 cm−1 range with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

To determine the percentage of elements in this CQD, Energy-Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDX) analysis was taken by MIRA3 (TESCAN). Dried samples were covered with a thin layer of gold, and EDS analysis of the sample was performed by maintaining a vacuum.

To identify and measure the percentage of elements present on the surface of CQDs and also prove doping of the elements, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied (Bes Tec, Germany, magnesium anode source and hu = 1253.6 eV).

Imaging of cancer cells done using an Eclipse Ti-s (Nikon) device. A histopathology examination performed using a biological microscope (BM-180 N). ELISA plate reader (Infinite® 200 PRO), BD FACSCalibur, and KODAK (4000 MM) devices used to calculate cytotoxicity, cell death, and distribution of drug formulations, respectively.

CQD was synthesized by the hydrothermal method. 5 g citric acid added to 20 ml water and stirred for 5 min. Then, 1.2 g MgOH added to the solution, and stirring continued for another 5 min. Next, 2.5 ml EDA added; it was placed in an autoclave and an oven at 200 °C for 4 h. The autoclave cooled down to room temperature. In order to purify CQD and reach to high QY, CQD solution passed through an aluminium oxide solid phase bed. In order to remove excess aluminium oxide powder, a centrifugation step at 10,000 rpm for 10 min performed. Then, the obtained solution was dialyzed with a 3500 Dalton dialysis bag under 1 L of deionized water for 24 h. The final solution freeze-dried for the characterization analyses [14].

Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY%= 54, η = 1.33) selected as a standard to calculate the QY of samples. CQD was dissolved in deionized water with different concentrations. All the absorbance values of the solutions at a 360 nm excitation wavelength measured with a UV-Vis spectrophotometer [14].

Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of all the sample solutions recorded by a fluorimeter at an excitation wavelength of λex= 360 nm. The integrated fluorescence intensity is the area under the PL curve in the wavelength range from 360 to 600 nm. Then, a graph plotted using the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance. QY calculated using the following equation:
(1)
∅ =
∅
st
× (
K
C
K

η

Where ϕ is the QY, KC, and Kst are integral gradients of fluorescence spectra in terms of absorption, and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” refers to the standard, and “C” refers to the CQD.
0.01 g FA was dissolved in 6 ml 0.01 M PBS buffer (pH=7.4). Then, 0.013 g of EDC and 0.0078 g of NHS added to the solution. The solution stirred overnight at room temperature in a dark place.

CQD (22 mg/ml) added to the solution and stirred for 12 h. In order to remove excess EDC, it was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm and purified using a 3500 Dalton dialysis bag against PBS for 1.5 h and then against deionized water for another 1.5 h [15].

In order to activate the amine groups of hyaluronic acid, 2 ml of an acidic solution of HA with pH= 4.5 was prepared using deionized water and HCl. Then, 3 mg EDC, 1.5 mg NHS, and 800 µl hyaluronic acid solution (300 µg/ml) were mixed. The solution was stirred for 2 h (the pH remained between 4.5 and 5). At the end of the reaction time, 2 ml CQD-FA was added to the solution, and the reaction continued for 30 min. In order to remove excess particles, the final solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag of 14000 Da against 400 ml deionized water for 2 h [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Epirubicin loading on the CQD-FA-HA complex
ce:section.ce:para: 8 mg CQD-FA-HA powder was added to 1 ml deionized water. Then, 2 ml PBS solution at pH= 7.4 was added to the CQD-FA-HA solution. Next, 1 ml EPI with a concentration of 400 µg/ml was added dropwise to the above solution and stirred at 1000 rpm for 12 h. The solution was dialyzed with a 3500 Da dialysis bag against 40 ml deionized water for 2 h. In order to measure the unloaded EPI, the water around the bag was analyzed with a standard calibration curve of EPI PL intensity at the λex=480 nm [17].

Drug loading content (DLC) and drug loading efficiency (DLE) obtained from the following equations [17].
(2)
DLE ( % ) =
(
m
EPI
−
m

)
m
EPI
× 100
(3)
DLC
( mg .
g
− 1
) =
(

EPI
−

)
m
NP
In the above equations, mEPI, mfree, and mNP are the content of EPI, the release amount of EPI around the dialysis bag, and the NP content, respectively.

2 ml of the final complex was sealed into a dialysis bag (3500 Da) and dialyzed against 20 ml phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4) and citrate buffer (pH = 5.5) and incubated in a shaker incubator (37 °C, 80 rpm, 72 h). At the specific times (0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24, 48, and 72 h), 500 µl of the solution outside the dialysis bag was removed and replaced with the fresh buffer. In order to correct the amount of drug released as a result of taking the sample at different times, the mass of the drug removed from the system due to sampling added to the mass of the drug released at the next time, and cumulative drug release plotted versus time [17].

In order to investigate the toxicity of CQD-FA-HA along with the drug EPI and compare it with the free drug, the Neutral red method was conducted on 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO (control) cells to measure the cytotoxicity of CQD-FA-HA, CQD-FA-HA-EPI, and free EPI. The cells were seeded at a density of 5000 in 96-well plates (RPMI 1640 culture medium enriched with 100 U/ml penicillin-streptomycin and 10% FBS) and incubated for 24 h (37 °C and 5% CO2). The culture medium drained, and 100 µl of fresh media containing CQD-FA-HA, CQD-FA-HA-EPI, and free EPI with different concentrations of EPI were added to each well. In the case of CQD-FA-HA, the cells incubated for another 24 h. Regarding CQD-FA-HA-EPI and free EPI, after 3 h, the culture medium was replaced with 200 µl fresh medium and incubated for 48 h. The Neutral red method used to calculate the percentage of cell viability [18–20]. The Neutral red medium centrifuged at 1800 rpm for 10 min. Then, it transferred to a falcon, and 100 µl of this solution was added to each well. The cells placed in the incubator for 3 h. After that, cells were washed with 150 µl PBS, followed by the addition of 150 µl acidic destain solution to each well. The desired plate placed on the shaker for 10 min. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 540 nm using a microplate reader. Prism software applied for statistical analysis. The two-way ANOVA method used for this aim. P-values considered as: * for P-value< 0.05, * * for P-value< 0.01, * ** for P-value< 0.001, and * ** * for P-value< 0.0001.

To determine the CQDs ability in cell imaging, three cell lines 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO (control), were used. Cells were seeded in 96-well plates with a density of 3000 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. The culture medium replaced with 90 µl fresh medium plus 10 µl containing 1 mg/ml of different complexes in each well. The microplate was placed in an incubator for 3 h. Then, it washed 3 times with PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde [21]. By applying eclipse Ti-s (Nikon) microscope, UV filter was used in excitation wavelength of 360–380 nm and emission wavelength of 450 nm for CQD, and by changing the filter for EPI drug, imaging was done with excitation wavelength of 460–480 nm [22,23]. It should be noted that although UV wavelength is harmful for cells, but the duration of applying UV ray is very short, which does not cause any damage to the cells.

BD FACS Calibur equipped with a 488 nm laser in the FL2 channel was used to evaluate the cellular internalization of CQD-FA-HA-EPI and free EPI. Cancer cell lines 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO (control) seeded in 24-well plates with a density of 150,000 plus 150 µl CQD-FA-HA-EPI/free EPI + 350 µl culture medium. After 3 h, the medium removed, and cells washed with 500 µl PBS, and 100 µl trypsin was added to each well. The trypsin neutralized with a 300 µl culture medium. Cells centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 5 min. After that, the cell pellets were rinsed and suspended in PBS and subjected to flow cytometry. FlowJo-10.8.1 software used to analyze the data [17].

To evaluate the distribution of different formulations containing EPI in the body and the amount of accumulation within the tumor, the prepared complexes (free EPI, CQD-EPI, CQD-FA-EPI, and CQD-FA-HA-EPI) injected into mice through the tail vein. The dose of EPI determined as 0.6 mg/kg of mouse body weight. Then, in vitro fluorescent imaging was performed at 6 and 24 h after injecting the targeted and non-targeted formulations and PBS as a negative control group. After 6 and 24 h of injection, mice euthanized using carbon dioxide. The desired organs, including the liver, kidney, spleen, heart, lung, and tumor, were removed under sterile conditions. They washed three times with PBS, and placed in 6 cm petri dishes covered with foil. Finally, the petri dishes placed in the device compartment, and fluorescent imaging was performed at λ = 540 nm, which is related to the wavelength of the EPI drug [24–26].

To investigate the in vivo function of the samples, 25 BALB/c inbred female mice (aged 6–8 weeks, and weighing 18–20 g) used. The animals randomly divided into five groups (5 mice in each group), including:
1)
Free EPI
2)
PBS group as a control group
3)
CQD-EPI group
4)
CQD-FA-EPI group and
5)
CQD-FA-HA-EPI group

The injection of the 4T1 cell line with a cell density of 5 × 105 considered to create a tumor. 150 µl of cells were injected subcutaneously into each animal. After approximately 2 weeks, when the tumor volume reached about 50 mm3, the mice were ready to receive the complex. EPI calculated as 0.6 mgEPI/ kg of body weight, and a single dose administered intravenously. Tumor volume was monitored every 3 days for 21 days. The tumor volume measured by the following equation:
(4)
Tumor
volume =
1 2
( width × length × height )

The systemic toxicity of the complexes was verified by harvesting the main organs, including the liver, spleen, heart, and tumor tissue, 21 days after treatment. The tissues fixed in 20 ml of 10% formaldehyde. The slides of tissues with 5 µm thickness were prepared and stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H & E). Finally, tissue changes examined using an optical microscope [22,27].

To ensure the successful synthesis of CQD and calculate the QY, the UV–visible absorption spectrum, and the fluorescence emission spectrum, were investigated at different CQD concentrations ( Fig. 1a).

The relationship between the spectrum of absorption and electron transfer of the core and shell of CQD is such that wavelengths less than 300 nm (λ = 250 nm in this study) are related to π-π * electron transitions in bonding orbitals related to CC double bonds of SP2 aromatic carbons [28]. The sequence of the absorption spectrum from 300 to 400 nm (λ = 348 nm in this study) is related to the electron transfers in non-bonding n-π * orbitals of CO bonds, which occurs in carbonyl compounds. Absorption bands above 400 nm originate from transitions of surface states with lone electron pairs [29,30].

One of the effective parameters in fluorescent carbon quantum dots, which has a direct relationship with surface states, is concentration [31]. According to Fig. 1b, in concentrations below 1.95 ppm, with the increase in the CQD concentration, the emission in long wavelengths increases. On the other hand, with an excessive increase in the CQD concentration, the amount of emission decreases due to the reabsorption effect (Fig. 1c). In high concentrations, by reducing the distance between emission centers on the CQD surface, the interaction between particles increases. As a result, the emission shift to red, and the fluorescent emission decreases. On the contrary, in low concentrations, by increasing the distance between particles, the interactions decreases, and fluorescent emission increases [32,33].

Moreover, to further investigate the optical properties of carbon quantum dots, the amount of fluorescence emission with different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 400 nm was investigated. According to Fig. 1d, with the increase in the excitation wavelength, the emission peak gradually shifted to longer wavelengths [34]. Therefore, the amount of emission of this CQD is dependent on the excitation wavelength, and this phenomenon caused by different energy levels with different surface states including different CQDs functional groups [35,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the operating conditions for CQD synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the maximum fluorescence emission and to use this carbon quantum dot for cell imaging, the effects of various parameters on the QY, including pyrolysis temperature, time, and pH, investigated.

According to the Table 1, with the increase in temperature from 180° to 220°C, the QY first increased and then decreased. At a temperature of 180 °C, the QY was 46.75%, and by increasing the temperature to 200 °C and increasing the process of carbonization and nucleation, the QY reached 68.52%. When the temperature increased above 200 °C, the QY decreased from 68.52% to 52.65%. One of the reasons for reducing the intensity of fluorescence emission is the excessive increase in the carbonization process, followed by the increase in the production of carbon particles and other side products. QY generally depends on factors such as surface states and the carbonization process. At a temperature of 180 °C, the major QY caused by nucleation due to the carbonization process. Meanwhile, at the temperature of 200 °C, the QY increased due to the synergy of carbon nucleation and surface states of carbon quantum dots [37].

In addition, the fluorescence band is extensive at high temperatures due to the strong electron-electron and weak electron-phonon interactions. Simultaneous doping of the CQD surface by Mg and N atoms carried out due to the temperature increase in the non-radiative channel's surface states [38,39]. Molecules are unstable in the excited and tend to return to the ground state through radiative and non-radiative processes. With the excessive temperature increase to 220 °C, more non-radiative channels activated. More excited electrons returned to the ground state through this non-radiative process, and then the fluorescence emission intensity and QY decreased [40,41]. At this stage, the temperature of 200 °C selected as the optimum value.

In the next step, the effect of pyrolysis time on the QY was investigated. In this regard, the synthesis of CQDs was carried out under a hydrothermal temperature of 200 °C at different times of 2, 4, 6, and 8 h. According to Table 1, increasing the time from 2 to 4 h led to an increase in the quantum efficiency, and from 4 h onwards, a decrease in the QY observed. Increase the carbonization process time enhances the QY. In long-term continuous heating, due to the excessive increase of the carbonization process and the growth of carbon nuclei, the QY decreases [42]. At 2 h, the QY caused by surface states, and at this time, the carbonization process is incomplete. The increase in QY at 4 h caused by the synergistic effects of surface states and the carbonization process. As the reaction time increases, the surface states are weakened, and the growth of the carbon nucleus is strengthened, thus leading to a decrease in the QY [37,39]. At this stage, 4 h was selected as the optimum pyrolysis time.

The pH-dependent behavior of CQD surface is closely related to deprotonation-protonation of the functional groups. Deprotonation may lead to electrostatic doping and a change in the fermi level of CQDs [43]. The pH-dependent behavior attributed to the carboxylic (-COOH) and amine (-NH2) functional groups on the CQD surface. -COOH and -NH2 have electron acceptor and electron donor properties, respectively. The -COOH group at acidic pH can withdraw electrons from the conduction band of the carbon core and stop the electron-hole recombination and decrease the fluorescence [40]. According to the Table 1, by the increase of pH from 5 to 9 and the increase of the carboxylic functional group, the efficiency of electron withdrawal decreases, thereby increasing the QY [44,45]. However, with an excessive increase in pH from 9 to 11, hydrogen bonds are formed between carboxyl groups, and the quantum efficiency decreases along with the accumulation of carbon quantum dots [40]. In addition, at acidic pH, the protonated form of -NH3 + occurs. It may reduce electron-donating efficiency [40].

Finally, pH= 9 selected as the optimum value. At the optimum conditions as T = 200 °C, time= 4 h, and pH= 9, QY was 89.44%, which is an excellent value compared to other studies. For example, Zhou et al. in 2022 synthesized carbon quantum dots using hydrochloric acid and phenyl diamine precursors under hydrothermal conditions. The quantum efficiency of this carbon quantum dot reported to be 58.65% [46]. In 2023, Jan et al. synthesized nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots using methionine and phenyl diamine precursors under hydrothermal conditions. They estimated the quantum efficiency to be 20% [47].

According to Fig. 2a, the FE-SEM image of CQDs is shown with high magnification on a scale of 200 nm. As the figure shows spherical particles with an approximate size of 50 nm can be seen. However, TEM analysis gives more accurate analysis than FE-SEM for particle size measurement.

In the next step, the CQDs size was investigated using TEM analysis. According to Fig. 2(b, c), the size distribution of carbon quantum dots reported between 3 and 13 nm, and the average particle size was between 6 and 7 nm. Moreover, aggregation and adhesion between particles were not observed.

HR-TEM analysis was carried out to investigate the surface structure and distance between the crystal lattice of CQDs. Fig. 3a shows the spherical and uniform structure of CQDs at the scale of 50 nm [14]. The histogram of particle size distribution between 4 and 10 nm and average particle size distribution of 6.5–7.5 are reported in the diagram of Fig. 3b. The crystal lattice spacing of 0.229 nm (2d = 2.748 / 12 = 0.229 nm) corresponds to the (100) diffraction plane in graphite carbon, which confirms the structure of graphite-like CQDs (Fig. 3c) [45]. In addition, the SAED pattern of CQDs shows rings and a few bright spots, which correspond to amorphous structures ( Fig. 4d). All the above results show that CQDs have been successfully synthesized under hydrothermal conditions and have a graphitic structure. These observations agree with a similar study published in 2021 [48].

The crystallographic structure of the synthesized CQDs investigated by XRD analysis. In Fig. 4a, the XRD pattern at 2Ɵ = 25° shows a flat and wide curve. This indicates the amorphous structure of the synthesized CQD. It is in agreement with similar studies [45,48].

EDS analysis determined the percentage of elements in this carbon quantum dot. According to the table and graph obtained from EDS analysis (Fig. 4b, c), the product is mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements with 33.60, 48.93, and 10.61 wt%, respectively. The main reason for the presence of the oxygen element in the product is related to the hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dot. The presence of nitrogen in the product surfaces can attribute to the amine functional groups. Magnesium element is also present in the CQD structure with 6.86 wt%.

To determine the chemical composition of the surface and confirm the simultaneous doping of the CQD sample with Mg and N atoms, XPS analysis of the sample investigated. The full scan XPS (survey) spectrum in Fig. 5a showed four specific peaks in the binding energy of 286.02 eV, 399.67 eV, 532.75 eV, and 51.25 eV, which were related to C 1 s and N 1 s, O 1 s, and Mg 2p, respectively [14,49,50]. This curve approves the successful synthesis and doping of nitrogen and magnesium in the Mg/N-CQD compound.

The composition of these four elements was calculated and presented in Fig. 5a. The atomic percentage of elements calculated as carbon: 54.46%, nitrogen: 7.44%, oxygen: 36.82%, and magnesium: 1.29%. The XPS high-resolution C 1 s spectrum shows a clear characteristic peak at 288.88 eV, which confirms the presence of the CO functional group in Mg/N-CQD (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, the peaks at 286.84 eV show the presence of C−O and C−N groups, peak at 285.59 eV shows the presence of C−C group and peak at 284.89 eV is related to the presence of CC group in this sample [51,52]. XPS spectrum of high-resolution O 1 s in Fig. 5c shows four peaks at 531/20 eV, 531/95 eV, 532/80 eV, and 533/39 eV, which respectively assigned to the functional groups of C−O, CO, N − O and O−H [53,54].

Fig. 5d shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s. According to the figure, successive deconvolutions were used in the spectrum to determine the main types of nitrogen groups present in the Mg/N-CQD sample. In general, five types of nitrogen groups determined in this sample: graphitic at 400/87 eV, pyrrolic at 399/66 eV, pyridinic at 391/7 eV, and in addition, two small signals at 397/04 eV and 403/04 eV that can probably attribute to the presence of pyridone and N-Oxide groups, respectively [55,56].

Usually, there are three different types of compounds with nitrogen atoms, namely graphitic, pyridinic, and pyrrolic nitrogen, covalently bound to graphene layers. In addition to these three types, two other types with less probability, including nitrogen oxide (N-Oxide) and nitrogen amide (Pyridone), are also given in the references, which are observed in nitrogen doping processes in carbon layers (N-doped) (Fig. 5e) [57,58].

To ensure the binding of folic acid and hyaluronic acid to the CQDs, UV, FTIR, and zeta potential analyses performed. It is predicted that the binding of FA and HA is through covalent bonding by activating the amine groups on the CQD surface and the carboxyl groups in FA and HA [15,59]. The characteristic absorption peak of FA at 282 nm, and HA at 250 nm, appeared in the absorption spectra of CQD-FA and CQD-FA-HA, which shows that FA and HA have been successfully covalently bound to the CQDs surface ( Fig. 6a) [60,61].

Fig. 6b shows the FTIR spectra for 4 samples of FA, CQD, CQD-FA, and CQD-FA-HA. According to the shape of the absorption peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 1712 cm−1, they can be attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and CO, respectively. The presence of these bonds indicates the presence of active groups such as amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, on the CQDs surface. These groups cause the hydrophilic properties and high solubility of CQDs in water. In the CQD-FA spectrum, the peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 1740 cm−1 attributed to N-H/O-H and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. This confirms the amide bonds on the surface and the successful binding of folic acid to the CQD [62]. The peaks at 1511 cm−1, 1411 cm−1, and 1031 cm−1, are attributed to N-H, CC, and C-N vibrations, respectively [15]. In the spectrum of CQD-FA-HA, after conjugating HA on the CQD-FA, a new small peak appeared at 1609 cm−1, and 2850 cm−1, which indicates the presence of an amide group (-NHCO-), and stretching vibration of C-H related to the HA, respectively. The presence of amide groups proves the successful binding of CQD-FA with HA [62,63].

The zeta potential of CQD, CQD-FA, and CQD-FA-HA were − 18.60, − 23.22, and − 26.52 mV, respectively. The increase in negative charge indicates that the CQD surface covered by negatively charged FA and HA [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and in vitro drug release
ce:section.ce:para:
EPI loading on the CQD-FA-HA complex was approved by calculating the DLE and DLC values as 86% and 41 mg/g, respectively. The possible mechanisms of drug loading on CQD-FA-HA can prove by several reasons:
i.
The possibility of drug loading by creating a bond between the carboxyl groups on the CQD surface and the amine group in EPI.
ii.
Through the electrostatic interaction due to the negative charge of the CQD’ carboxylic groups and the positive charge of the EPI’ amine group. It approved using the zeta potential test.
iii.
The possibility of π-π interaction due to the presence of aromatic rings in CQD and EPI.

Considering the sensitivity of EPI release to pH, drug release was investigated at pH= 7.4 and pH= 5.5 during 72 h. As shown in Fig. 6c, the release values of EPI at pH= 7.4 and pH= 5.5 after 72 h are reported as 42% and 86%, respectively. This indicates that the amount of EPI release increases at lower pH, which is due to the enhancement of amine groups protonation of EPI, followed by the separation of EPI from the surface of CQD-FA-HA. It increases the solubility of the drug in an acidic environment. The increase in pH-sensitive EPI release indicates the excellent performance of the complex in the treatment of cancer cells because the natural environment of lysosomes, endosomes, and tumors is acidic. In addition, the release of EPI continued slowly for 72 h. This shows that the selected system not only has good selectivity for the drug release site but also leads to the long-term release of the drug and can increase the duration of the drug’s therapeutic effects [17,64,65].

In vitro cytotoxicity evaluation of CQD-FA-HA was performed on 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO cell lines using the Neutral red method. In this part, the toxicity of CQD at different concentrations showed low toxicity for all three cell lines 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO at a concentration of 2 mg/ml after 24 h ( Fig. 7a). Then, in vitro cytotoxicity tests were performed on 4T1, MCF-7, and CHO cell lines to verify the biocompatibility of the CQD-FA-HA complex and evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of the encapsulated EPI (Fig. 7b, c, d). As evident from the figures, the rate of cell death in CHO cells (FR-) is higher in the free drug than the targeted drug with CQD-FA-HA. While according to Figs. 7c-d, the targeted drug in 4T1 and MCF-7 cells with FA and HA receptors worked well, and viability decreased compared to the free drug. In addition, cell death by applying the drug in dually targeted CQD-FA-HA is higher than in CQD-FA. It confirms the excellent performance of the dual-targeted system in inhibiting the growth of cancer cells expressing the desired receptors.

In order to examine the cell imaging properties of various complexes, fluorescence microscopy imaging was performed for 4T1 and MCF-7 as cancer cell lines expressing FA/HA receptors and CHO as control cells without the receptors. As seen in Fig. 8, CQDs without FA and HA entered all three cell lines and emitted blue fluorescence under excitation at 480 nm laser. The performance of the targeted drug in 4T1 and MCF-7 cell lines shows the triumphant entry of CQD modified with FA and HA into the cytoplasm of the cells. Moreover, the emission of CQD fluorescence in these complexes shows the excellent performance of these materials in cellular imaging applications. However, CHO cells could not recognize the binding of FA and HA groups to the CQD surface because FR and CD44 are not overexpressed on the membrane of CHO cells [27].

The intracellular uptake of CQD-FA-HA-EPI and free EPI investigated by flow cytometry analysis. According to Fig. 9, flow cytometry performed on 4T1 and MCF-7 overexpressing FA/HA receptors and CHO cells without the receptors for CQD-FA-HA-EPI and free EPI complexes. As seen in Fig. 10, the fluorescence intensity of the free EPI recorded for all three cell lines (orange). For CQD-FA-HA-EPI (red), 4T1 and MCF-7 cells emitted stronger fluorescence than free EPI. However, the cellular uptake of free EPI was higher than that of CQD-FA-HA-EPI in CHO cells. These results indicated that the CQD-FA-HA-EPI complex acts in a targeted manner in identifying FR- and CD44-positive cancer cells and enters the cytoplasm of the cells through the receptors. As a result, the targeted CQD-FA-HA-EPI complex can significantly increase the effectiveness of the treatment [66].

Fluorescence imaging used to investigate drug accumulation in the body. In this regard, different organs of treated mice in various groups, including liver, kidney, spleen, heart, lung, and tumor, were dissected and examined after 6 and 24 h of injection. According to Fig. 10 a,c, no obvious fluorescence signal was observed after 6 and 24 h in the targeted drug carrier groups. Moreover, the fluorescence signal in the tumor was higher in the targeted groups in the form of double modification with FA and HA compared to the targeted drug carrier modified with only FA. It is due to the excellent performance of the targeted drug in the body that the accumulation and release of the drug were done only at the target sites such as tumor cells. The diagrams in Fig. 10 (b,d) represents the fluorescence signals quantitatively and approve fluorescence imaging observations.

In order to investigate the effects of treatments with different synthesized complexes in vivo, the tumor volume in different groups was measured once every 3 days for 21 days, and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 11. Statistical differences of changes in tumor volumes on day 21 verified using Ordinary one-way ANOVA by applying GraphPad Prism software.

Based on the statistical results, there was no significant difference between the tumor size in the group receiving EPI and the group receiving CQD-EPI (P value>0.05). It confirms that EPI binding to CQDs does not reduce CQD’s toxicity. However, applying FA for targeting FR changed the tumor size in the CQD-FA-EPI group significantly compared to free EPI (P value< 0.05). It confirms the positive effects of FA targeting.

Next, by adding HA to the CQD-FA-EPI complex, it can be seen that the difference in tumor size of the group receiving the new combination CQD-FA-HA-EPI is more significant compared to the group receiving free EPI (P value<0.01). This confirms the positive effects of FA and HA in dual targeting.

After 21 days of treatments with a single dose injection of CQD-EPI, CQD-FA-EPI, CQD-FA-HA-EPI, free EPI, and the control group receiving PBS, histological examination was performed for various organs, including the liver, spleen, heart, and tumor. According to Fig. 12, the histopathological observations summarized as follows [24].
•
CQD-EPI group: Pathology of liver tissue showed degeneration of liver cells, necrosis with neutrophils (red arrows), and a small number of mononuclear cells. Light microscopic observation of the spleen tissue in this group showed severe degeneration and necrosis of white pulp and red pulp associated with trabecular degeneration. In the heart tissue, moderate necrotic degeneration of cardiac myocytes associated with interfibrillar bleeding (black arrow) and the infiltration of inflammatory cells and lymphocytes in the tumor tissue observed.
•
CQD-FA-EPI and CQD-FA-HA-EPI groups: Light microscopic observations did not show any liver cell degeneration or necrosis and liver tissue inflammation in these groups. Moreover, the spleen section in these two groups showed the standard tissue structure of red and white pulp. The histopathological studies of the heart tissue indicated that the cardiomyocytes were healthy, clear, and orderly, and there was no congestion, bleeding, inflammation, or necrosis. Disruption of the tumor cells with necrosis was more in the CQD-FA-HA-EPI group than the CQD-FA-EPI group.
•
Free EPI group: Histological examination of liver tissue showed severe necrosis of liver cells with neutrophils and a small number of mononuclear cells. In the spleen tissue, moderate degeneration and necrosis of the white pulp, along with severe necrosis of the red pulp, as well as trabecular degeneration, were observed. Necrotic degeneration of cardiac myocytes (red arrow) associated with the infiltration of inflammatory cells (black arrow) showed in the heart tissue. Infiltration of inflammatory cells and lymphocytes also observed in the tumor tissue.
•
Control group (PBS): The histological examination of the liver tissue showed a patchy area of liver cell necrosis combined with multinuclear and mononuclear inflammatory cells. In spleen tissue, moderate degeneration and necrosis of white pulp and red pulp associated with trabecular degeneration. In the heart, the cardiomyocytes are almost transparent and orderly, and slight necrosis observed. There were no inflammatory cells with necrosis in the tumor tissue.

According to the observed results, the organs in the targeted groups are healthy, and there were no complications. In contrast, in the control (PBS group) and other groups without the targeted agents, the tissues in different organs were damaged, which may be due to the side effects of the free drug or the 4T1 cancer cell line of mice.

The aim of this study was the synthesis of CQDs with high QY for drug delivery and cell imaging applications. At first, the synthesis of CQDs with high photoluminescence properties performed by optimizing the pyrolysis conditions. In the synthesis process, due to precursor types, the surface of CQDs simultaneously doped with Mg and N atoms. Then, dual targeting of CQDs with FA and HA performed. Characterization results approved the successful formation of CQD and conjugation of FA and HA. In vitro drug release study approved pH dependency of nanocarriers in drug release media suitable for application in acidic tumor cell environment. Cytotoxicity study showed higher toxicity of CQD-FA-HA-EPI in 4T1 and MCF-7 cell lines.

Furthermore, fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry analyses approved the ability of the nanocarrier for cell imaging. Ex vivo studies confirmed higher drug accumulation in tumor tissue in comparison to other organs, which predicts lower side effects of this novel complex. Finally, the performance of the designed carrier was approved in vivo with a significant reduction of tumor volume in BALB/c inbred female mice with lower damage to main organs compared to free EPI. In conclusion, this novel complex can be proposed as a new platform for breast cancer treatment and cell imaging, although further investigations needed before its clinical applications.
This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Dr. Farhadian was the supervisor of the project, the main conceptual ideas, and the proof outline. Ms. Ziaee worked out almost all of the technical details, such as sample preparation and characterization and wrote the initial draft of the paper. Dr. M. Matin supervised and verified the results of the cellular study and in vivo tests. Dr. Abnous checked the results of the cellular imaging and in vivo analysis. Mr. Ali Khooshnod helped with sample preparation and analysis. Dr. Yaghoobi helped in the cell culture stage. All authors reviewed the manuscript.
